perturbation.original_text: The Recipient acknowledges that it is aware (and, if applicable, that its Representatives who are apprised of this matter have been advised) that the United States securities laws prohibit any person who has material non-public information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of such company, or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities. The Recipient agrees that, to the extent that it has actually received Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement that constitutes material non-public information, it will not trade any securities of CEDC or any of its Subsidiaries prior to the termination of this Agreement and that it will not use any Confidential Information in contravention of the United States securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient acknowledges that it is aware (and, if applicable, that its Representatives who are apprised of this matter have been advised) that the United States securities laws may restrict trading on inside information. The Recipient agrees that it will not use any Confidential Information in a way that management deems inappropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit prohibition on trading securities based on material non-public information and weakens the commitment to comply with securities laws, substituting a vague requirement to avoid actions 'management deems inappropriate'. This creates a legal risk by potentially allowing the recipient to violate insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Insider Trading Laws - Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the compliance with insider trading laws, specifically Rule 10b-5. The original text acknowledges the prohibition, while the modified text only mentions that securities laws 'may restrict trading' and replaces the explicit ban on trading with a subjective standard of 'management deems inappropriate.' This creates ambiguity and could be interpreted as allowing insider trading if management does not object.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the commitment to comply with securities laws, specifically Rule 10b-5, creating a legal contradiction. The original text explicitly prohibits trading securities based on material non-public information, referencing United States securities laws, while the modified text only mentions that securities laws 'may restrict trading' and replaces the explicit ban with a subjective standard of 'management deems inappropriate.' This contradicts 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits employing any device to defraud or engaging in any act that operates as a fraud or deceit in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The revised language introduces ambiguity and weakens the adherence to insider trading regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and take effect as an enforceable contract in accordance with the laws of the State of New York governing such agreements, without regard to conflicts-of-law principles thereof that would require applicability of any other law. The parties hereto agree that any dispute between them relating to this Agreement will be resolved solely in the manner set forth in clause (i) below: (i) Each of the parties hereto irrevocably submits to the jurisdiction of the United States District Court located in the State of New York and in the Borough of Manhattan, and all appellate courts relating thereto, for the purpose of any suit, action, proceeding or judgment relating to or arising out of this Agreement. Service of process in connection with any such suit, action or proceeding may be served on each party hereto anywhere in the world by the same methods as are specified for the giving of notices under this Agreement. Each party hereto irrevocably waives any objection to the laying of venue of any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such courts and irrevocably waives any claim that any such suit, action or proceeding brought in an inconvenient forum. EACH OF THE PARTIES HERETO WAIVES ANY RIGHT TO REQUEST A TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY LITIGATION WITH RESPECT TO THIS AGREEMENT AND REPRESENTS THAT COUNSEL HAS BEEN CONSULTED SPECIFICALLY AS TO THIS WAIVER.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and take effect as an enforceable contract in accordance with the laws of the State of New York governing such agreements, without regard to conflicts-of-law principles thereof that would require applicability of any other law. The parties hereto agree that any dispute between them relating to this Agreement will be resolved solely in the manner set forth in clause (i) below: (i) Each of the parties hereto irrevocably submits to the jurisdiction of the United States District Court located in the State of New York and in the Borough of Manhattan, and all appellate courts relating thereto, for the purpose of any suit, action, proceeding or judgment relating to or arising out of this Agreement. Service of process in connection with any such suit, action or proceeding may be served on each party hereto anywhere in the world by the same methods as are specified for the giving of notices under this Agreement. Each party hereto irrevocably waives any objection to the laying of venue of any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such courts and irrevocably waives any claim that any such suit, action or proceeding brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the jury trial waiver introduces a legal risk. While parties can waive their right to a jury trial, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. By omitting the explicit waiver and the representation that counsel was consulted, the agreement is more susceptible to a challenge arguing that the waiver was not valid, therefore contradicting the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit waiver of a jury trial and the statement that counsel has been consulted, the agreement potentially violates the Seventh Amendment, which guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. Removing the explicit waiver increases the risk that a court would find the waiver invalid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the jury trial waiver and the statement about consulting counsel. The Law Explanation indicates this could violate the Seventh Amendment, which guarantees the right to a jury trial. The scraped snippet from the Legal Information Institute states that the Seventh Amendment preserves the right to a jury trial, absent waiver by the parties. By removing the explicit waiver, the agreement now has an omission that increases the risk a court could find the waiver invalid, contradicting the legally protected right to a jury trial unless knowingly waived.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall use reasonable efforts to provide written notice to CEDC of any use or disclosure of Confidential Information in breach of this Agreement of which Recipient is aware, including without limitation written details regarding the circumstances and the identity of the individuals or entities who as a result received or may have received access to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may, at its discretion, provide notice to CEDC of any use or disclosure of Confidential Information in breach of this Agreement of which Recipient is aware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall use reasonable efforts to provide written notice' to 'may, at its discretion, provide notice' significantly weakens the obligation to report breaches. This creates a legal risk because it removes the mandatory aspect of reporting, potentially allowing the recipient to conceal breaches of confidentiality, contradicting common law duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (New York Law)
perturbation.law_citation: New York Uniform Commercial Code, Section 1-304
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the requirement to report breaches of confidentiality from 'shall use reasonable efforts' to a discretionary 'may', the modified text potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law (UCC § 1-304). The original text obligates the Recipient to make a good-faith effort to inform CEDC of any breaches, while the modified text allows the Recipient to remain silent even if they are aware of a breach, undermining the purpose of the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, specifically as it relates to the obligation to report breaches of confidentiality. The original text mandated a 'reasonable effort' to provide written notice, while the changed text makes reporting discretionary ('may, at its discretion'). UCC § 1-304, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, imposes an 'obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement' on every contract. By removing the mandatory aspect of reporting, the modified text allows the Recipient to potentially conceal breaches, thus undermining the good faith performance of the confidentiality agreement, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Recipient agrees that, upon written request by CEDC, all Confidential Information (and all copies, summaries and notes of the contents or parts thereof) and all Company property received by the Recipient shall, as soon as reasonably practicable, be either returned to CEDC or, to the extent technically practicable, destroyed (at the Recipient’s or its Representatives’ option), except that Recipient and its Representatives may retain copies of Confidential Information as is required to comply with applicable law or regulation or professional standards or internal compliance requirements. The Recipient’s obligations under Section 2 hereof shall survive the return of such tangible embodiments of Confidential Information until termination of this Agreement. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, neither Recipient nor any of its Representatives shall be required to delete electronically stored Confidential Information to the extent such deletion would be technologically impracticable or inconsistent with the archival records retention policy of the Recipient or its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Recipient agrees that, upon request by CEDC, all Confidential Information (and all copies, summaries and notes of the contents or parts thereof) and all Company property received by the Recipient shall be retained by the Recipient at its discretion. The Recipient’s obligations under Section 2 hereof shall survive the return of such tangible embodiments of Confidential Information until termination of this Agreement. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, neither Recipient nor any of its Representatives shall be required to delete electronically stored Confidential Information to the extent such deletion would be technologically impracticable or inconsistent with the archival records retention policy of the Recipient or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: This completely removes the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon request, instead stating that the recipient shall retain it at its discretion. This creates a legal risk by contradicting standard trade secret protection practices, where return or destruction of confidential information is a key remedy for misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement to return or destroy confidential information and replacing it with a discretionary retention policy, the modified text weakens trade secret protection and potentially violates the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S.C. § 1836. The DTSA provides remedies for trade secret misappropriation, including injunctive relief to prevent further use or disclosure of the trade secret. The original text facilitated compliance with the return/destruction component of the DTSA, while the modified text actively impedes it, as it grants the Recipient sole discretion to retain the information. This impedes CEDC's ability to recover its trade secrets if misappropriation occurs and pursue remedies under the DTSA.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts standard trade secret protection practices. The original text required the recipient to return or destroy confidential information upon request, aligning with remedies available under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as stated in the Law Explanation. The modified text, however, grants the recipient sole discretion to retain the information, effectively eliminating the return/destruction obligation. The scraped snippet of 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(1) discusses private civil actions for misappropriation of trade secrets, indicating that owners of trade secrets can bring civil action if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in interstate or foreign commerce. This suggests that retaining the information at the recipient's discretion could impede the owner's ability to recover the trade secret and pursue remedies. Therefore, the modification creates a direct contradiction by removing a crucial component of trade secret protection.
